title: I
html:
I

It was quite by half-past fiveÔªø‚Äîafter the two men had been together in Madame de Vionnet‚Äôs drawing-room not more than a dozen minutesÔªø‚Äîthat Chad, with a look at his watch and then another at their hostess, said genially, gaily: ‚ÄúI‚Äôve an engagement, and I know you won‚Äôt complain if I leave him with you. He‚Äôll interest you immensely; and as for her,‚Äù he declared to Strether, ‚ÄúI assure you, if you‚Äôre at all nervous, she‚Äôs perfectly safe.‚Äù

He had left them to be embarrassed or not by this guarantee, as they could best manage, and embarrassment was a thing that Strether wasn‚Äôt at first sure Madame de Vionnet escaped. He escaped it himself, to his surprise; but he had grown used by this time to thinking of himself as brazen. She occupied, his hostess, in the Rue de Bellechasse, the first floor of an old house to which our visitors had had access from an old clean court. The court was large and open, full of revelations, for our friend, of the habit of privacy, the peace of intervals, the dignity of distances and approaches; the house, to his restless sense, was in the high homely style of an elder day, and the ancient Paris that he was always looking forÔªø‚Äîsometimes intensely felt, sometimes more acutely missedÔªø‚Äîwas in the immemorial polish of the wide waxed staircase and in the fine boiseries, the medallions, mouldings, mirrors, great clear spaces, of the greyish-white salon into which he had been shown. He seemed at the very outset to see her in the midst of possessions not vulgarly numerous, but hereditary cherished charming. While his eyes turned after a little from those of his hostess and Chad freely talkedÔªø‚Äînot in the least about him, but about other people, people he didn‚Äôt know, and quite as if he did know themÔªø‚Äîhe found himself making out, as a background of the occupant, some glory, some prosperity of the First Empire, some Napoleonic glamour, some dim lustre of the great legend; elements clinging still to all the consular chairs and mythological brasses and sphinxes‚Äô heads and faded surfaces of satin striped with alternate silk.

The place itself went further backÔªø‚Äîthat he guessed, and how old Paris continued in a manner to echo there; but the post-revolutionary period, the world he vaguely thought of as the world of Chateaubriand, of Madame de Sta√´l, even of the young Lamartine, had left its stamp of harps and urns and torches, a stamp impressed on sundry small objects, ornaments and relics. He had never before, to his knowledge, had present to him relics, of any special dignity, of a private orderÔªø‚Äîlittle old miniatures, medallions, pictures, books; books in leather bindings, pinkish and greenish, with gilt garlands on the back, ranged, together with other promiscuous properties, under the glass of brass-mounted cabinets. His attention took them all tenderly into account. They were among the matters that marked Madame de Vionnet‚Äôs apartment as something quite different from Miss Gostrey‚Äôs little museum of bargains and from Chad‚Äôs lovely home; he recognised it as founded much more on old accumulations that had possibly from time to time shrunken than on any contemporary method of acquisition or form of curiosity. Chad and Miss Gostrey had rummaged and purchased and picked up and exchanged, sifting, selecting, comparing; whereas the mistress of the scene before him, beautifully passive under the spell of transmissionÔªø‚Äîtransmission from her father‚Äôs line, he quite made up his mindÔªø‚Äîhad only received, accepted and been quiet. When she hadn‚Äôt been quiet she had been moved at the most to some occult charity for some fallen fortune. There had been objects she or her predecessors might even conceivably have parted with under need, but Strether couldn‚Äôt suspect them of having sold old pieces to get ‚Äúbetter‚Äù ones. They would have felt no difference as to better or worse. He could but imagine their having feltÔªø‚Äîperhaps in emigration, in proscription, for his sketch was slight and confusedÔªø‚Äîthe pressure of want or the obligation of sacrifice.

The pressure of wantÔªø‚Äîwhatever might be the case with the other forceÔªø‚Äîwas, however, presumably not active now, for the tokens of a chastened ease still abounded after all, many marks of a taste whose discriminations might perhaps have been called eccentric. He guessed at intense little preferences and sharp little exclusions, a deep suspicion of the vulgar and a personal view of the right. The general result of this was something for which he had no name on the spot quite ready, but something he would have come nearest to naming in speaking of it as the air of supreme respectability, the consciousness, small, still, reserved, but none the less distinct and diffused, of private honour. The air of supreme respectabilityÔªø‚Äîthat was a strange blank wall for his adventure to have brought him to break his nose against. It had in fact, as he was now aware, filled all the approaches, hovered in the court as he passed, hung on the staircase as he mounted, sounded in the grave rumble of the old bell, as little electric as possible, of which Chad, at the door, had pulled the ancient but neatly-kept tassel; it formed in short the clearest medium of its particular kind that he had ever breathed. He would have answered for it at the end of a quarter of an hour that some of the glass cases contained swords and epaulettes of ancient colonels and generals; medals and orders once pinned over hearts that had long since ceased to beat; snuffboxes bestowed on ministers and envoys; copies of works presented, with inscriptions, by authors now classic. At bottom of it all for him was the sense of her rare unlikeness to the women he had known. This sense had grown, since the day before, the more he recalled her, and had been above all singularly fed by his talk with Chad in the morning. Everything in fine made her immeasurably new, and nothing so new as the old house and the old objects. There were books, two or three, on a small table near his chair, but they hadn‚Äôt the lemon-coloured covers with which his eye had begun to dally from the hour of his arrival and to the opportunity of a further acquaintance with which he had for a fortnight now altogether succumbed. On another table, across the room, he made out the great Revue; but even that familiar face, conspicuous in Mrs.¬ÝNewsome‚Äôs parlours, scarce counted here as a modern note. He was sure on the spotÔªø‚Äîand he afterwards knew he was rightÔªø‚Äîthat this was a touch of Chad‚Äôs own hand. What would Mrs.¬ÝNewsome say to the circumstance that Chad‚Äôs interested ‚Äúinfluence‚Äù kept her paper-knife in the Revue? The interested influence at any rate had, as we say, gone straight to the pointÔªø‚Äîhad in fact soon left it quite behind.

She was seated, near the fire, on a small stuffed and fringed chair one of the few modern articles in the room, and she leaned back in it with her hands clasped in her lap and no movement, in all her person, but the fine prompt play of her deep young face. The fire, under the low white marble, undraped and academic, had burnt down to the silver ashes of light wood, one of the windows, at a distance, stood open to the mildness and stillness, out of which, in the short pauses, came the faint sound, pleasant and homely, almost rustic, of a plash and a clatter of sabots from some coach-house on the other side of the court. Madame de Vionnet, while Strether sat there, wasn‚Äôt to shift her posture by an inch. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt think you seriously believe in what you‚Äôre doing,‚Äù she said; ‚Äúbut all the same, you know, I‚Äôm going to treat you quite as if I did.‚Äù

‚ÄúBy which you mean,‚Äù Strether directly replied, ‚Äúquite as if you didn‚Äôt! I assure you it won‚Äôt make the least difference with me how you treat me.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù she said, taking that menace bravely and philosophically enough, ‚Äúthe only thing that really matters is that you shall get on with me.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh but I don‚Äôt!‚Äù he immediately returned.

It gave her another pause; which, however, she happily enough shook off. ‚ÄúWill you consent to go on with me a littleÔªø‚ÄîprovisionallyÔªø‚Äîas if you did?‚Äù

Then it was that he saw how she had decidedly come all the way; and there accompanied it an extraordinary sense of her raising from somewhere below him her beautiful suppliant eyes. He might have been perched at his doorstep or at his window and she standing in the road. For a moment he let her stand and couldn‚Äôt moreover have spoken. It had been sad, of a sudden, with a sadness that was like a cold breath in his face. ‚ÄúWhat can I do,‚Äù he finally asked, ‚Äúbut listen to you as I promised Chadwick?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh but what I‚Äôm asking you,‚Äù she quickly said, ‚Äúisn‚Äôt what Mr.¬ÝNewsome had in mind.‚Äù She spoke at present, he saw, as if to take courageously all her risk. ‚ÄúThis is my own idea and a different thing.‚Äù

It gave poor Strether in truthÔªø‚Äîuneasy as it made him tooÔªø‚Äîsomething of the thrill of a bold perception justified. ‚ÄúWell,‚Äù he answered kindly enough, ‚ÄúI was sure a moment since that some idea of your own had come to you.‚Äù

She seemed still to look up at him, but now more serenely. ‚ÄúI made out you were sureÔªø‚Äîand that helped it to come. So you see,‚Äù she continued, ‚Äúwe do get on.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh but it appears to me I don‚Äôt at all meet your request. How can I when I don‚Äôt understand it?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt isn‚Äôt at all necessary you should understand; it will do quite well enough if you simply remember it. Only feel I trust youÔªø‚Äîand for nothing so tremendous after all. Just,‚Äù she said with a wonderful smile, ‚Äúfor common civility.‚Äù

Strether had a long pause while they sat again face to face, as they had sat, scarce less conscious, before the poor lady had crossed the stream. She was the poor lady for Strether now because clearly she had some trouble, and her appeal to him could only mean that her trouble was deep. He couldn‚Äôt help it; it wasn‚Äôt his fault; he had done nothing; but by a turn of the hand she had somehow made their encounter a relation. And the relation profited by a mass of things that were not strictly in it or of it; by the very air in which they sat, by the high cold delicate room, by the world outside and the little plash in the court, by the First Empire and the relics in the stiff cabinets, by matters as far off as those and by others as near as the unbroken clasp of her hands in her lap and the look her expression had of being most natural when her eyes were most fixed. ‚ÄúYou count upon me of course for something really much greater than it sounds.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh it sounds great enough too!‚Äù she laughed at this.

He found himself in time on the point of telling her that she was, as Miss Barrace called it, wonderful; but, catching himself up, he said something else instead. ‚ÄúWhat was it Chad‚Äôs idea then that you should say to me?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh his idea was simply what a man‚Äôs idea always isÔªø‚Äîto put every effort off on the woman.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe ‚Äòwoman‚ÄôÔªø‚Äî?‚Äù Strether slowly echoed.

‚ÄúThe woman he likesÔªø‚Äîand just in proportion as he likes her. In proportion tooÔªø‚Äîfor shifting the troubleÔªø‚Äîas she likes him.‚Äù

Strether followed it; then with an abruptness of his own: ‚ÄúHow much do you like Chad?‚Äù

‚ÄúJust as much as thatÔªø‚Äîto take all, with you, on myself.‚Äù But she got at once again away from this. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve been trembling as if we were to stand or fall by what you may think of me; and I‚Äôm even now,‚Äù she went on wonderfully, ‚Äúdrawing a long breathÔªø‚Äîand, yes, truly taking a great courageÔªø‚Äîfrom the hope that I don‚Äôt in fact strike you as impossible.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs at all events, clearly,‚Äù he observed after an instant, ‚Äúthe way I don‚Äôt strike you.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù she so far assented, ‚Äúas you haven‚Äôt yet said you won‚Äôt have the little patience with me I ask forÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúYou draw splendid conclusions? Perfectly. But I don‚Äôt understand them,‚Äù Strether pursued. ‚ÄúYou seem to me to ask for much more than you need. What, at the worst for you, what at the best for myself, can I after all do? I can use no pressure that I haven‚Äôt used. You come really late with your request. I‚Äôve already done all that for myself the case admits of. I‚Äôve said my say, and here I am.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, here you are, fortunately!‚Äù Madame de Vionnet laughed. ‚ÄúMrs.¬ÝNewsome,‚Äù she added in another tone, ‚Äúdidn‚Äôt think you can do so little.‚Äù

He had an hesitation, but he brought the words out. ‚ÄúWell, she thinks so now.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you mean by thatÔªø‚Äî?‚Äù But she also hung fire.

‚ÄúDo I mean what?‚Äù

She still rather faltered. ‚ÄúPardon me if I touch on it, but if I‚Äôm saying extraordinary things, why, perhaps, mayn‚Äôt I? Besides, doesn‚Äôt it properly concern us to know?‚Äù

‚ÄúTo know what?‚Äù he insisted as after thus beating about the bush she had again dropped.

She made the effort. ‚ÄúHas she given you up?‚Äù

He was amazed afterwards to think how simply and quietly he had met it. ‚ÄúNot yet.‚Äù It was almost as if he were a trifle disappointedÔªø‚Äîhad expected still more of her freedom. But he went straight on. ‚ÄúIs that what Chad has told you will happen to me?‚Äù

She was evidently charmed with the way he took it. ‚ÄúIf you mean if we‚Äôve talked of itÔªø‚Äîmost certainly. And the question‚Äôs not what has had least to do with my wishing to see you.‚Äù

‚ÄúTo judge if I‚Äôm the sort of man a woman canÔªø‚Äî?‚Äù

‚ÄúPrecisely,‚Äù she exclaimedÔªø‚Äî‚Äúyou wonderful gentleman! I do judgeÔªø‚ÄîI have judged. A woman can‚Äôt. You‚Äôre safeÔªø‚Äîwith every right to be. You‚Äôd be much happier if you‚Äôd only believe it.‚Äù

Strether was silent a little; then he found himself speaking with a cynicism of confidence of which even at the moment the sources were strange to him. ‚ÄúI try to believe it. But it‚Äôs a marvel,‚Äù he exclaimed, ‚Äúhow you already get at it!‚Äù

Oh she was able to say. ‚ÄúRemember how much I was on the way to it through Mr.¬ÝNewsomeÔªø‚Äîbefore I saw you. He thinks everything of your strength.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I can bear almost anything!‚Äù our friend briskly interrupted. Deep and beautiful on this her smile came back, and with the effect of making him hear what he had said just as she had heard it. He easily enough felt that it gave him away, but what in truth had everything done but that? It had been all very well to think at moments that he was holding her nose down and that he had coerced her: what had he by this time done but let her practically see that he accepted their relation? What was their relation moreoverÔªø‚Äîthough light and brief enough in form as yetÔªø‚Äîbut whatever she might choose to make it? Nothing could prevent herÔªø‚Äîcertainly he couldn‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîfrom making it pleasant. At the back of his head, behind everything, was the sense that she wasÔªø‚Äîthere, before him, close to him, in vivid imperative formÔªø‚Äîone of the rare women he had so often heard of, read of, thought of, but never met, whose very presence, look, voice, the mere contemporaneous fact of whom, from the moment it was at all presented, made a relation of mere recognition. That was not the kind of woman he had ever found Mrs.¬ÝNewsome, a contemporaneous fact who had been distinctly slow to establish herself; and at present, confronted with Madame de Vionnet, he felt the simplicity of his original impression of Miss Gostrey. She certainly had been a fact of rapid growth; but the world was wide, each day was more and more a new lesson. There were at any rate even among the stranger ones relations and relations. ‚ÄúOf course I suit Chad‚Äôs grand way,‚Äù he quickly added. ‚ÄúHe hasn‚Äôt had much difficulty in working me in.‚Äù

She seemed to deny a little, on the young man‚Äôs behalf, by the rise of her eyebrows, an intention of any process at all inconsiderate. ‚ÄúYou must know how grieved he‚Äôd be if you were to lose anything. He believes you can keep his mother patient.‚Äù

Strether wondered with his eyes on her. ‚ÄúI see. That‚Äôs then what you really want of me. And how am I to do it? Perhaps you‚Äôll tell me that.‚Äù

‚ÄúSimply tell her the truth.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd what do you call the truth?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, any truthÔªø‚Äîabout us allÔªø‚Äîthat you see yourself. I leave it to you.‚Äù

‚ÄúThank you very much. I like,‚Äù Strether laughed with a slight harshness, ‚Äúthe way you leave things!‚Äù

But she insisted kindly, gently, as if it wasn‚Äôt so bad. ‚ÄúBe perfectly honest. Tell her all.‚Äù

‚ÄúAll?‚Äù he oddly echoed.

‚ÄúTell her the simple truth,‚Äù Madame de Vionnet again pleaded.

‚ÄúBut what is the simple truth? The simple truth is exactly what I‚Äôm trying to discover.‚Äù

She looked about a while, but presently she came back to him. ‚ÄúTell her, fully and clearly, about us.‚Äù

Strether meanwhile had been staring. ‚ÄúYou and your daughter?‚Äù

‚ÄúYesÔªø‚Äîlittle Jeanne and me. Tell her,‚Äù she just slightly quavered, ‚Äúyou like us.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd what good will that do me? Or rather‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîhe caught himself upÔªø‚Äî‚Äúwhat good will it do you?‚Äù

She looked graver. ‚ÄúNone, you believe, really?‚Äù

Strether debated. ‚ÄúShe didn‚Äôt send me out to ‚Äòlike‚Äô you.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh,‚Äù she charmingly contended, ‚Äúshe sent you out to face the facts.‚Äù

He admitted after an instant that there was something in that. ‚ÄúBut how can I face them till I know what they are? Do you want him,‚Äù he then braced himself to ask, ‚Äúto marry your daughter?‚Äù

She gave a headshake as noble as it was prompt. ‚ÄúNoÔªø‚Äînot that.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd he really doesn‚Äôt want to himself?‚Äù

She repeated the movement, but now with a strange light in her face. ‚ÄúHe likes her too much.‚Äù

Strether wondered. ‚ÄúTo be willing to consider, you mean, the question of taking her to America?‚Äù

‚ÄúTo be willing to do anything with her but be immensely kind and niceÔªø‚Äîreally tender of her. We watch over her, and you must help us. You must see her again.‚Äù

Strether felt awkward. ‚ÄúAh with pleasureÔªø‚Äîshe‚Äôs so remarkably attractive.‚Äù

The mother‚Äôs eagerness with which Madame de Vionnet jumped at this was to come back to him later as beautiful in its grace. ‚ÄúThe dear thing did please you?‚Äù Then as he met it with the largest ‚ÄúOh!‚Äù of enthusiasm: ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs perfect. She‚Äôs my joy.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôm sure thatÔªø‚Äîif one were near her and saw more of herÔªø‚Äîshe‚Äôd be mine.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen,‚Äù said Madame de Vionnet, ‚Äútell Mrs.¬ÝNewsome that!‚Äù

He wondered the more. ‚ÄúWhat good will that do you?‚Äù As she appeared unable at once to say, however, he brought out something else. ‚ÄúIs your daughter in love with our friend?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh,‚Äù she rather startlingly answered, ‚ÄúI wish you‚Äôd find out!‚Äù

He showed his surprise. ‚ÄúI? A stranger?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh you won‚Äôt be a strangerÔªø‚Äîpresently. You shall see her quite, I assure you, as if you weren‚Äôt.‚Äù

It remained for him none the less an extraordinary notion. ‚ÄúIt seems to me surely that if her mother can‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúAh little girls and their mothers today!‚Äù she rather inconsequently broke in. But she checked herself with something she seemed to give out as after all more to the point. ‚ÄúTell her I‚Äôve been good for him. Don‚Äôt you think I have?‚Äù

It had its effect on himÔªø‚Äîmore than at the moment he quite measured. Yet he was consciously enough touched. ‚ÄúOh if it‚Äôs all youÔªø‚Äî!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, it may not be ‚Äòall,‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù she interrupted, ‚Äúbut it‚Äôs to a great extent. Really and truly,‚Äù she added in a tone that was to take its place with him among things remembered.

‚ÄúThen it‚Äôs very wonderful.‚Äù He smiled at her from a face that he felt as strained, and her own face for a moment kept him so. At last she also got up. ‚ÄúWell, don‚Äôt you think that for thatÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúI ought to save you?‚Äù So it was that the way to meet herÔªø‚Äîand the way, as well, in a manner, to get offÔªø‚Äîcame over him. He heard himself use the exorbitant word, the very sound of which helped to determine his flight. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll save you if I can.‚Äù
